seo.title: Free Business Calculators for Restaurants & Retail | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free online calculators for restaurants and small businesses — GST, profit margin, markup, break-even, food cost %, discount, loan EMI and table turnover rate. No signup needed.

hero.headline: Business calculators, built for operators
hero.subheadline: Quick, free tools for the numbers you check every week — pricing, margins, tax and more. No signup, just answers.

items.name: GST Calculator
items.shortDescription: Add or remove GST from any amount in a tap, for any tax slab.

seo.title: GST Calculator — Add or Remove GST Online Free | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free GST calculator to add GST to a base amount or remove GST from a final price, for any GST rate. Get the tax amount and total instantly.

hero.headline: GST Calculator
hero.subheadline: Add GST to a base price or pull GST out of a final price — pick a rate and get the split instantly.

about.headline: Why use a GST calculator
paragraphs: GST shows up everywhere in a business's day-to-day — quoting a price to a customer, checking a supplier invoice, or working out what to enter on a bill. Doing the maths by hand for every slab is slow and easy to get wrong, especially when you're moving between rates like 5%, 12%, 18% and 28%.

This calculator works both ways. Enter a base amount to see the GST and total with tax added, or enter a final amount to see what the base price and tax were before GST was included. It's the same calculation Setu POS runs automatically on every bill, available here as a free, standalone tool.

items.question: How do I calculate GST on an amount?
items.answer: Multiply the base amount by the GST rate and divide by 100 to get the GST amount, then add it to the base amount for the total. For example, ₹1,000 at 18% GST adds ₹180, for a total of ₹1,180.

items.question: How do I remove GST from a final price to find the base price?
items.answer: Divide the final amount by (1 + GST rate/100). For a final amount of ₹1,180 at 18% GST, the base price is ₹1,180 ÷ 1.18 = ₹1,000.

items.question: What are the common GST slabs in India?
items.answer: The most common GST slabs are 5%, 12%, 18% and 28%, though some goods and services fall outside these or are exempt. Always check the rate that applies to what you're billing.

items.question: Does Setu POS calculate GST automatically?
items.answer: Yes. Setu POS has a built-in tax engine that applies the correct GST automatically on every bill, so you don't need to calculate it manually for each order.

cta.headline: Let Setu POS handle tax on every bill
cta.subtext: Setu POS applies the right GST automatically on every order — no manual calculation, no mistakes.

items.name: Profit Margin Calculator
items.shortDescription: Work out your profit margin and markup % from cost and selling price.

seo.title: Profit Margin Calculator — Free Online Tool | Setu Technology
seo.description: Calculate profit margin and markup percentage from your cost price and selling price. Free profit margin calculator for retail and restaurant businesses.

hero.headline: Profit Margin Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter your cost price and selling price to see your profit, margin % and markup % at once.

about.headline: Why margin and markup both matter
paragraphs: Profit margin tells you what share of your selling price is actual profit — useful for checking whether a product or dish is genuinely worth selling at its current price. Markup tells you how much you've added on top of cost — useful when you're setting a price from scratch.

The two numbers look similar but aren't the same: a 50% markup on cost is only a 33% margin on the selling price. This calculator shows both together so you can sanity-check pricing decisions without mixing the two up.

items.question: What is the formula for profit margin?
items.answer: Profit margin % = (Selling Price − Cost Price) ÷ Selling Price × 100. It measures profit as a percentage of the selling price.

items.question: What's the difference between margin and markup?
items.answer: Margin is profit divided by selling price; markup is profit divided by cost price. They use the same profit figure but different denominators, so a given profit amount produces a higher markup % than margin %.

items.question: What's a good profit margin for a small business?
items.answer: It varies widely by industry — restaurants often run 10-15% net margin, while retail margins vary by category. Compare your margin against your own historical numbers and industry benchmarks rather than a single universal target.

items.question: Should I price based on margin or markup?
items.answer: Most businesses set prices using markup on cost, then check the resulting margin to confirm it meets their profit target. Using both avoids underpricing items that look fine by one measure but not the other.

cta.headline: See your real margins, automatically
cta.subtext: Setu POS tracks cost and sales together, so you always know your margin without doing the maths yourself.

items.name: Markup Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find the selling price you need to hit a target markup over cost.

seo.title: Markup Calculator — Find Your Selling Price | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free markup calculator. Enter your cost price and target markup percentage to instantly find the selling price and profit per unit.

hero.headline: Markup Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter your cost and target markup % to get the selling price you should charge.

about.headline: Pricing from cost upward
paragraphs: Cost-plus pricing — taking your cost and adding a fixed markup — is the simplest way to price a new product or menu item. The hard part is doing it consistently across dozens of SKUs or dishes without a calculator open in another tab for every one.

This tool flips the usual margin question around: instead of checking the margin on a price you've already picked, you tell it the markup you want and it gives you the price to charge, along with the profit per unit at that price.

items.question: What is the markup formula?
items.answer: Selling Price = Cost Price × (1 + Markup % ÷ 100). For example, a ₹100 cost item with a 40% markup sells at ₹140.

items.question: Is a 50% markup the same as a 50% margin?
items.answer: No. A 50% markup on a ₹100 cost gives a ₹150 selling price, which works out to a 33.3% margin, not 50%. Markup and margin are calculated on different bases.

items.question: What markup should I use for retail products?
items.answer: It depends on the category, competition and how much value-add or service comes with the product. Many retailers start in the 30-50% markup range and adjust based on what sells and what the market will bear.

items.question: Can I use this for restaurant menu pricing too?
items.answer: Yes — enter your plate cost as the cost price and your target markup to get a starting menu price, then sanity-check it against the food cost percentage calculator.

cta.headline: Price every item with confidence
cta.subtext: Setu POS keeps cost and pricing data together so you can update prices without losing track of margin.

items.name: Break-Even Point Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find out how many units you need to sell to cover your costs.

seo.title: Break-Even Point Calculator — Free Online Tool | Setu Technology
seo.description: Calculate your break-even point in units and revenue. Free break-even calculator for restaurants, retail stores and small businesses.

hero.headline: Break-Even Point Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter your fixed costs, price and variable cost per unit to see exactly how much you need to sell to break even.

about.headline: Know your number before the month starts
paragraphs: Your break-even point is the level of sales where revenue exactly covers your costs — sell less and you're in a loss, sell more and every extra unit is pure contribution to profit. It's one of the most useful numbers in a business because it turns rent, salaries and other fixed costs into a single, concrete sales target.

Enter your monthly fixed costs (rent, salaries, subscriptions), your variable cost per unit (ingredients, packaging, per-order costs) and your selling price, and this calculator works out the units and revenue you need to hit before you're in profit.

items.question: What is the break-even point formula?
items.answer: Break-even units = Fixed Costs ÷ (Selling Price per unit − Variable Cost per unit). Multiply the result by the selling price to get break-even revenue.

items.question: What counts as a fixed cost vs a variable cost?
items.answer: Fixed costs stay roughly the same regardless of sales volume — rent, salaries, software subscriptions. Variable costs scale with each unit sold — ingredients, packaging, payment gateway fees per order.

items.question: Why does break-even matter for a new restaurant or store?
items.answer: It converts your overheads into a daily or monthly sales target, which makes it much easier to judge whether a location, menu price or staffing level is realistic before you commit to it.

items.question: What is contribution margin?
items.answer: Contribution margin is the selling price minus the variable cost per unit — the amount each sale contributes toward covering fixed costs. A higher contribution margin means you break even with fewer units sold.

cta.headline: Track sales against your break-even, daily
cta.subtext: Setu POS gives you real-time sales data, so you always know where you stand against your numbers.

items.name: Food Cost Percentage Calculator
items.shortDescription: Check if a dish's ingredient cost is in a healthy range for its menu price.

seo.title: Food Cost Percentage Calculator — Free for Restaurants | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free food cost percentage calculator for restaurants. Enter ingredient cost and menu price to find food cost % and profit per dish instantly.

hero.headline: Food Cost Percentage Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter a dish's ingredient cost and menu price to see its food cost % and profit per plate.

about.headline: The single number every menu price should pass
paragraphs: Food cost percentage is the share of a dish's menu price that goes toward its ingredients. Most restaurants aim to keep this somewhere between 28% and 35%, though it varies by cuisine, format and how much labour goes into a dish versus raw ingredient cost.

Run each dish on your menu through this calculator when you're setting prices or when ingredient costs change. A dish with a food cost % far outside your target range either needs a price change, a recipe change, or is intentionally priced as a loss-leader — but you should know which one it is.

items.question: How do you calculate food cost percentage?
items.answer: Food Cost % = (Ingredient Cost ÷ Menu Selling Price) × 100. A dish costing ₹70 in ingredients sold at ₹280 has a food cost of 25%.

items.question: What is a good food cost percentage for a restaurant?
items.answer: Most full-service and quick-service restaurants target 28-35% food cost, but high-end ingredients, beverages and bakery items can run lower or higher and still be profitable once labour and overhead are factored in.

items.question: Why is my food cost percentage higher than expected?
items.answer: Common causes are portion sizes drifting larger over time, ingredient price increases that prices haven't caught up to, wastage in the kitchen, or recipes that were never costed properly to begin with.

items.question: Does this account for labour cost?
items.answer: No — this calculator covers raw ingredient (food) cost only. Labour, packaging and overhead should be tracked separately alongside food cost when deciding final menu prices.

cta.headline: Cost every dish accurately, automatically
cta.subtext: Setu POS connects recipes to inventory, so food cost stays accurate as ingredient prices change.

items.name: Discount Calculator
items.shortDescription: Work out the final price and savings for any discount or offer.

seo.title: Discount Calculator — Final Price & Savings | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free discount calculator. Enter the original price and discount percentage to instantly see the final price and amount saved.

hero.headline: Discount Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter the original price and discount percentage to see the final price and exactly how much is saved.

about.headline: Useful for both sides of the counter
paragraphs: When you're running a sale or a coupon offer, you need to know the final price you'll actually charge — and what it costs you in margin. This calculator gives you both instantly, for any discount percentage.
It's also handy for checking a supplier's discount offer on a bulk order, or for quickly answering a customer's "how much is that after the discount" question without reaching for a phone calculator.

items.question: How do I calculate a discounted price?
items.answer: Discount Amount = Original Price × Discount % ÷ 100. Final Price = Original Price − Discount Amount. A ₹500 item at 20% off saves ₹100, for a final price of ₹400.

items.question: How do running a discount affect my profit margin?
items.answer: A discount reduces your selling price but not your cost, so it eats directly into margin. Check the profit margin calculator on the discounted price before running a promotion to make sure it's still worth selling at.

items.question: Can I stack two discounts together?
items.answer: Apply them one after another rather than adding the percentages — a 20% discount followed by a further 10% off is not a 30% total discount. Run the first discount through the calculator, then run the result through again for the second discount.

items.question: Can Setu POS apply discounts and coupons automatically at billing?
items.answer: Yes. Setu POS supports configurable coupons and discounts that apply automatically at the bill, so staff don't need to calculate offers manually at the counter.

cta.headline: Run offers without the manual maths
cta.subtext: Setu POS applies coupons and discounts automatically at billing, every time.

items.name: Business Loan EMI Calculator
items.shortDescription: Estimate your monthly EMI, total interest and total payment on a business loan.

seo.title: Business Loan EMI Calculator — Free Online Tool | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free business loan EMI calculator. Enter loan amount, interest rate and tenure to instantly see your monthly EMI, total interest and total repayment.

hero.headline: Business Loan EMI Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter the loan amount, interest rate and tenure to see your monthly EMI, total interest and total repayment.

about.headline: Know the monthly number before you sign
paragraphs: Whether it's a working capital loan, equipment financing or a line of credit to fund a new outlet, the EMI is the number that actually shows up against your cash flow every month. Comparing loan offers by interest rate alone can be misleading once tenure and processing costs are factored in.

Enter the loan amount, annual interest rate and tenure to get the EMI using the standard reducing-balance formula, along with the total interest you'll pay over the life of the loan — useful for comparing two offers side by side.

items.question: What is the EMI formula?
items.answer: EMI = P × r × (1 + r)^n ÷ ((1 + r)^n − 1), where P is the loan amount, r is the monthly interest rate (annual rate ÷ 12 ÷ 100), and n is the number of monthly instalments.

items.question: Does this include processing fees or other charges?
items.answer: No — this calculates EMI based on principal, interest rate and tenure only. Add any processing fees or charges separately when comparing the true cost of different loan offers.

items.question: Is the interest rate I should enter the same as the rate the bank quotes?
items.answer: Use the annual interest rate as quoted by the lender. If a lender quotes a flat rate instead of a reducing-balance rate, the actual EMI will differ from what this calculator shows, since flat-rate loans charge interest on the full principal throughout the tenure.

items.question: How does a shorter tenure affect total interest?
items.answer: A shorter tenure means a higher EMI but significantly less total interest paid, since less time passes with interest accruing on the outstanding balance. A longer tenure lowers the EMI but increases total interest paid over the loan.

cta.headline: Funding a new outlet? We can help you plan it
cta.subtext: Talk to us about what it takes to open and run a new location with Setu POS in place from day one.

items.name: Table Turnover Rate Calculator
items.shortDescription: See how many times each table turns over during service, and your average turn time.

seo.title: Table Turnover Rate Calculator — Free for Restaurants | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free table turnover rate calculator for restaurants. Enter covers served, number of tables and service hours to find turnover rate and average turn time.

hero.headline: Table Turnover Rate Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter covers served, number of tables and service hours to see your turnover rate and average turn time.

about.headline: The number behind a busy-looking dining room
paragraphs: Table turnover rate tells you how many times, on average, each table was used during a service period. It's a direct measure of how efficiently your floor is converting seats into covers — and during peak hours, it matters as much as footfall does.

Enter the total covers served, the number of tables you have, and the length of the service period, and this calculator works out your turnover rate and the average time each table was occupied per turn — useful for spotting whether slow turns are capping your revenue on busy nights.

items.question: How is table turnover rate calculated?
items.answer: Table Turnover Rate = Total Covers Served ÷ Number of Tables. If 120 covers were served across 20 tables during a service, the turnover rate is 6 — each table was used 6 times on average.

items.question: What is a good table turnover rate for a restaurant?
items.answer: It depends heavily on format — quick-service and casual dining aim for higher turnover (multiple turns per service), while fine dining intentionally runs lower turnover with longer dwell times and higher spend per cover. Compare against your own format and past performance.

items.question: How do I improve table turnover without rushing customers?
items.answer: Faster kitchen ticket times, efficient billing and payment, and staff trained to read natural pauses in a table's meal (rather than imposing a time limit) typically improve turnover more sustainably than rushing guests.

items.question: Can Setu POS track table status in real time?
items.answer: Yes. Setu POS shows live table status — open, occupied, billed — across your floor, so staff always know which tables are free without walking the room to check.

cta.headline: See every table's status, live
cta.subtext: Setu POS tracks table status in real time, so your floor staff always know what's free.

items.name: Take-Home Salary Calculator
items.shortDescription: See your monthly in-hand salary after PF, professional tax and other deductions from CTC.

seo.title: Take-Home Salary Calculator — CTC to In-Hand Salary | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free take-home salary calculator. Enter your CTC to estimate monthly in-hand salary after PF, professional tax and other standard deductions.
keywords: CTC to in hand salary calculator

hero.headline: Take-Home Salary Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter your annual CTC to estimate your monthly in-hand salary after standard deductions.

about.headline: What actually lands in your account each month
paragraphs: CTC (Cost to Company) is rarely the amount that lands in a bank account. Provident fund contributions, professional tax and other deductions come out before salary is paid, and the gap between CTC and in-hand pay surprises a lot of new hires — and a lot of owners budgeting for a new role.

This calculator gives a quick estimate of monthly take-home pay from an annual CTC figure, using typical PF and professional tax assumptions. Actual numbers depend on your specific salary structure (basic, HRA, allowances) and company policy, so treat this as a planning estimate rather than a payslip.

items.question: What is the difference between CTC and take-home salary?
items.answer: CTC is the total cost an employer bears for an employee, including basic pay, allowances, employer's PF contribution and other benefits. Take-home salary is what actually gets paid into the employee's bank account each month, after deductions like employee PF contribution, professional tax and income tax (if applicable).

items.question: Why is my take-home salary lower than CTC divided by 12?
items.answer: Because CTC includes employer contributions (like employer PF) that never reach the employee's bank account, and deductions like employee PF, professional tax and TDS are subtracted from gross pay before it's disbursed.

items.question: Is this calculator accurate for my exact salary slip?
items.answer: It's a planning estimate based on typical assumptions for PF and professional tax. Your actual take-home pay depends on your specific salary structure, state, and any additional deductions like income tax TDS, so use your company's payslip or HR for the exact figure.

items.question: Does this include income tax deduction?
items.answer: No — this calculator estimates deductions for PF and professional tax only. For an estimate that includes income tax, use the income tax calculator alongside this tool.

cta.headline: Staffing a new location? Plan the numbers first
cta.subtext: Setu POS gives you the sales and labour-cost data to know if a new hire pays for itself.

items.name: Income Tax Calculator
items.shortDescription: Estimate income tax under the old vs new regime for FY 2025-26.

seo.title: Income Tax Calculator (FY 2025-26) — Old vs New Regime | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free income tax calculator for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27). Compare old regime vs new regime tax and see which saves you more. Estimate only — verify against the latest Budget.
keywords: old vs new tax regime calculator

hero.headline: Income Tax Calculator
hero.subheadline: Compare old regime vs new regime tax on your income for FY 2025-26, and see which one works out cheaper.

about.headline: Old regime or new regime — know before you choose
paragraphs: India's income tax system lets most taxpayers choose between the old regime (more deductions, higher slab rates) and the new regime (fewer deductions, lower slab rates). The right choice depends entirely on how many deductions and exemptions you actually claim — there's no universal answer.

This calculator estimates tax payable under both regimes for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) using the latest slabs and standard deduction we have on record, including the Section 87A rebate. It does not account for surcharge on very high incomes, marginal-relief edge cases, or every possible deduction — and tax slabs are typically revised in the Union Budget each year, so always cross-check against the latest official slabs or a chartered accountant before filing or making a regime decision.

items.question: What are the new tax regime slabs for FY 2025-26?
items.answer: Under the new regime (FY 2025-26 / AY 2026-27): nil up to ₹4,00,000; 5% from ₹4,00,001-8,00,000; 10% from ₹8,00,001-12,00,000; 15% from ₹12,00,001-16,00,000; 20% from ₹16,00,001-20,00,000; 25% from ₹20,00,001-24,00,000; and 30% above ₹24,00,000. Tax slabs change with each Union Budget, so confirm against the latest official notification before relying on this for filing.

items.question: What is the Section 87A rebate?
items.answer: It's a rebate that brings tax liability to zero for taxpayers under a certain taxable income threshold. Under the new regime for FY 2025-26, the rebate generally applies up to ₹12,00,000 taxable income, with marginal relief tapering it just above that threshold. This calculator applies a simplified version and doesn't model every marginal-relief edge case.

items.question: Should I choose the old regime or new regime?
items.answer: It depends on how much you claim in deductions — HRA, 80C investments, home loan interest, and so on. If your eligible deductions are large, the old regime often works out cheaper; if you claim few deductions, the new regime's lower slab rates usually win. Compare both using this calculator with your actual numbers.

items.question: Is this calculator a substitute for filing advice from a CA?
items.answer: No. This is a simplified estimate for planning purposes only — it excludes surcharge, cess nuances for some cases, and several itemised deductions. For your actual tax filing or a regime decision with real money at stake, verify the latest slabs and consult a qualified chartered accountant.

cta.headline: Run your business, not your tax spreadsheet
cta.subtext: Setu POS keeps your sales and expense data organised, so tax season is less of a scramble.

items.name: Gratuity Calculator
items.shortDescription: Estimate gratuity payable under the Payment of Gratuity Act based on salary and years of service.

seo.title: Gratuity Calculator — Payment of Gratuity Act | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free gratuity calculator. Enter last drawn salary and years of service to estimate gratuity payable under the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972.

hero.headline: Gratuity Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter last drawn salary and years of service to estimate gratuity payable on exit.

about.headline: What gratuity is owed, and when
paragraphs: Gratuity is a lump-sum payment employers owe employees who've completed at least 5 years of continuous service, under the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972. It's calculated from an employee's last drawn salary and total years of service — not their full career earnings.

This calculator applies the standard formula used for employees covered under the Act, and is useful for both employees estimating what they're owed on exit and owners budgeting for the gratuity liability of long-tenured staff. The statutory ceiling on gratuity is revised periodically by the government, so confirm the current cap before relying on this for a final settlement figure.

items.question: What is the formula for gratuity calculation?
items.answer: Gratuity = (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Number of Years of Service) ÷ 26, for employees covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act. Salary here typically means basic pay plus dearness allowance.

items.question: Am I eligible for gratuity?
items.answer: Generally, employees who've completed 5 or more years of continuous service with the same employer are eligible, with some exceptions (such as death or disablement, where the 5-year condition doesn't apply).

items.question: Is there a maximum limit on gratuity?
items.answer: Yes — the Payment of Gratuity Act sets a statutory ceiling on tax-exempt gratuity, which the government revises periodically. Check the current limit before treating this calculator's output as a final, capped figure.

items.question: How is 'years of service' counted if I worked 7 years and 8 months?
items.answer: Under the Act, a period of service of more than 6 months past a completed year is typically rounded up to the next full year. So 7 years 8 months would usually be treated as 8 years for the calculation.

cta.headline: Planning your staffing budget?
cta.subtext: Setu POS gives you the sales data to plan staffing and long-term payroll costs around what your outlet can support.

items.name: Online Order Commission Calculator
items.shortDescription: See your real payout after platform commission, packaging and payment gateway charges on an online order.

seo.title: Online Food Delivery Commission Calculator | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free online order commission calculator for restaurants. Enter order value, platform commission and other charges to see your real payout per order.

hero.eyebrow: Free Tool — Online Order Commission Calculator
hero.headline: Online Order Commission Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter the order value and platform charges to see exactly what you take home from an online order.

about.headline: What an online order actually pays you
paragraphs: Listed commission rates rarely tell the whole story. Once you add packaging cost, payment gateway charges and GST on the commission itself, the gap between the order value a customer pays and what actually lands in your account can be 30% or more.

Enter the order value and the charges that apply on your aggregator account to see the real payout per order and your effective take-home percentage. Run a few of your common order sizes through this to see whether online orders are as profitable as your dine-in or takeaway business once all the deductions are in.

items.question: How do food delivery platforms calculate commission?
items.answer: Most platforms charge a commission as a percentage of the order value, and may charge GST on top of that commission. Packaging charges and payment gateway fees are typically separate and reduce your payout further.

items.question: Why is my actual payout so much lower than the order value?
items.answer: Commission, GST on commission, packaging cost and payment gateway fees all come out of the order value before you're paid. On a typical order these combined deductions commonly run 25-35% of the order value, depending on your platform and category.

items.question: Should I price items differently for delivery platforms?
items.answer: Many restaurants do build a delivery-specific markup into their aggregator menu prices to offset commission, since dine-in and delivery have very different cost structures. Use this calculator to check whether your current delivery prices leave you a sustainable margin.

items.question: Can Setu POS manage orders from multiple delivery platforms?
items.answer: Yes. Setu POS can bring in orders from multiple aggregators into a single view, so your kitchen doesn't have to juggle separate tablets for every platform.

cta.headline: Manage every aggregator from one screen
cta.subtext: Setu POS brings orders from all your delivery platforms into a single kitchen view.

items.name: Recipe Costing Calculator
items.shortDescription: Cost out a recipe ingredient by ingredient to find true cost per serving.

seo.title: Recipe Costing Calculator — Cost Per Serving | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free recipe costing calculator for restaurants. Add ingredients and quantities to find total recipe cost, cost per serving and a suggested menu price.

hero.headline: Recipe Costing Calculator
hero.subheadline: Add each ingredient and quantity to find the true cost of a recipe, per serving.

about.headline: Cost the recipe, not just the headline ingredients
paragraphs: Most menu pricing mistakes come from costing a dish on its 2-3 main ingredients and guessing the rest. Oil, spices, garnish and packaging are individually small but add up — and that gap compounds across hundreds of orders a month.

Add every ingredient in a recipe with its quantity and unit cost to get an accurate total cost and cost per serving. Once you know the real cost, use it alongside the food cost percentage calculator to set a menu price that protects your margin.

items.question: Why should I cost every ingredient instead of just the main ones?
items.answer: Small ingredients like oil, spices, sauces and garnish are individually cheap but add up across a recipe, and across hundreds of plates sold each month that gap can meaningfully erode margin if it's never accounted for.

items.question: How many servings should I divide the total cost by?
items.answer: Divide by the actual number of servings or plates the recipe yields, not the number of ingredients used. If a batch recipe makes a base used across 10 plates, use 10 as the serving count.

items.question: Does this include labour or overhead cost?
items.answer: No — this calculates raw ingredient cost only. Add labour and overhead separately, and consider both alongside ingredient cost when setting a final menu price.

items.question: Can I save this for every dish on my menu?
items.answer: This tool doesn't store data between sessions, so note down the cost per serving for each dish as you go. Setu POS can store recipe costs against menu items permanently and update automatically as ingredient prices change.

cta.headline: Recipes that update cost automatically
cta.subtext: Setu POS links recipes to live ingredient prices, so cost per plate stays accurate without manual recalculation.

items.name: Liquor Pour Cost Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find your pour cost percentage and profit on every drink you pour.

seo.title: Liquor Pour Cost Calculator — Bar Cost Control | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free liquor pour cost calculator for bars and restaurants. Enter bottle cost, pour size and selling price to find pour cost % and profit per drink.

hero.eyebrow: Free Tool — Liquor Pour Cost Calculator
hero.headline: Liquor Pour Cost Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter bottle cost, pour size and selling price to see your pour cost % and profit per drink.

about.headline: The bar's version of food cost percentage
paragraphs: Pour cost is to a bar what food cost percentage is to a kitchen — the share of a drink's selling price that goes toward the liquor itself. It's the number that tells you whether your drink pricing and pour sizes are actually profitable, especially once you factor in how many pours a single bottle yields.

Enter the bottle cost, bottle size, the pour size you actually serve, and the selling price per drink to get pour cost %, profit per pour and how many pours you should get from each bottle. Run your top sellers through this whenever a supplier changes a price.

items.question: How is pour cost percentage calculated?
items.answer: Pour Cost % = (Cost per Pour ÷ Selling Price per Pour) × 100, where Cost per Pour = Bottle Cost ÷ (Bottle Size ÷ Pour Size).

items.question: What is a good pour cost percentage?
items.answer: Most bars target 18-24% pour cost on spirits, though it varies by category — premium and craft offerings often run a bit higher in exchange for a higher price point and perceived value.

items.question: Why does my actual pour cost not match my target?
items.answer: Over-pouring is the most common cause — even an extra 5-10ml per drink compounds quickly across a busy night. Inconsistent pour sizes, spillage, and complimentary or comped drinks also widen the gap between target and actual pour cost.

items.question: How many pours should I get from one bottle?
items.answer: Pours per Bottle = Bottle Size ÷ Pour Size. A standard 750ml bottle poured at 30ml per drink should yield 25 pours — track actual pours against this to spot over-pouring or wastage.

cta.headline: Control bar costs the same way you control food cost
cta.subtext: Setu POS tracks inventory consumption against sales, so over-pouring and wastage show up fast.

items.name: Tip Split Calculator
items.shortDescription: Split a shared tip pool fairly across staff by hours, shares or equally.

seo.title: Tip Split Calculator — Fair Tip Pooling for Staff | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free tip split calculator. Add staff and hours or shares to split a tip pool fairly — equal split, by hours worked, or by points.

hero.headline: Tip Split Calculator
hero.subheadline: Add your staff and split a shared tip pool equally, by hours worked, or by points/shares.

about.headline: Tip pooling that staff can see is fair
paragraphs: Splitting tips manually gets messy fast once you're accounting for different shift lengths or roles. A server who worked a full double shift and one who covered the last two hours shouldn't get equal shares — but agreeing on exactly how to weight it is where most disputes start.

This calculator splits a tip pool three ways: equally across everyone, proportionally by hours worked, or by a points/shares system where you assign each role or person a weight. Add your staff, pick a method, and the split updates instantly.

items.question: What's the difference between an equal split and a points-based split?
items.answer: An equal split divides the pool evenly regardless of role or hours. A points-based split assigns each person a weight (points or shares) reflecting role, seniority or hours, and divides the pool proportionally to those weights — common when front-of-house and support roles are pooled together.

items.question: Should kitchen staff be included in a tip pool with servers?
items.answer: Tip pooling rules and norms vary by region and by local labour regulations — some jurisdictions restrict who can be included in a tip pool. Check what's permitted where you operate before deciding who's eligible.

items.question: How do I split tips fairly by hours worked?
items.answer: Use the 'by hours' method — enter each person's hours for the shift or period, and the pool is divided proportionally, so someone who worked twice as long receives roughly twice the share.

items.question: Can I use this for a single shift or a whole week's tips?
items.answer: Either — enter the total tip pool for whatever period you're splitting (a shift, a day, a week) along with the hours or shares for that same period.

cta.headline: Payroll and tips, without the side spreadsheet
cta.subtext: Setu POS tracks shift hours and sales by staff member, so you've always got the numbers tip splits need.

items.name: Inventory Turnover Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find how many times your inventory sells through in a period, and how many days of stock you're holding.

seo.title: Inventory Turnover Ratio Calculator — Free Tool | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free inventory turnover calculator for retail businesses. Enter cost of goods sold and average inventory to find turnover ratio and days of inventory on hand.

hero.headline: Inventory Turnover Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter cost of goods sold and average inventory value to see your turnover ratio and days of stock on hand.

about.headline: Cash tied up on a shelf is cash you can't use elsewhere
paragraphs: Inventory turnover tells you how many times you sell through your average stock in a given period. A low turnover ratio usually means cash sitting in slow-moving stock; a very high one can mean you're at risk of stockouts. Neither extreme is automatically good — the right number depends on your category and how easily you can restock.

Enter your cost of goods sold for a period (month or year) and your average inventory value over the same period to get your turnover ratio and the equivalent number of days of stock you're holding. Track this over time rather than in isolation — a falling turnover ratio is often the earliest sign of slow-moving or dead stock building up.

items.question: What is the inventory turnover ratio formula?
items.answer: Inventory Turnover Ratio = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Inventory Value, for the same period. Average inventory is typically (Opening Inventory + Closing Inventory) ÷ 2.

items.question: What is a good inventory turnover ratio?
items.answer: It varies widely by category — perishables and fast fashion turn over much faster than furniture or electronics. Compare your ratio against your own historical trend and category benchmarks rather than a single universal number.

items.question: What does 'days inventory outstanding' mean?
items.answer: It's the turnover ratio converted into days — roughly how many days of sales your current average inventory represents. It's calculated as 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover Ratio, and a rising number signals stock is moving more slowly than before.

items.question: Why is my inventory turnover dropping?
items.answer: Common causes are overstocking ahead of demand, slow-moving SKUs accumulating, seasonal demand dips, or pricing that's no longer competitive enough to move stock at the previous pace.

cta.headline: Want early access to Setu Retail?
cta.subtext: Tell us about your retail business and we'll notify you as soon as Setu Retail launches, with inventory tracking built in.

items.name: Stock Reorder Point Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find the exact stock level that should trigger your next reorder.

seo.title: Stock Reorder Point Calculator — Avoid Stockouts | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free reorder point calculator for retail. Enter average daily sales, lead time and safety stock to find the stock level that should trigger reordering.

hero.eyebrow: Free Tool — Stock Reorder Point Calculator
hero.headline: Stock Reorder Point Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter average daily sales, supplier lead time and safety stock to find when you should reorder.

about.headline: Reorder before you run out, not after
paragraphs: A reorder point is the stock level that should trigger a new purchase order — set it too low and you risk running out before the new stock arrives; set it too high and you're tying up cash in excess inventory.

Enter your average daily sales for an item, how many days your supplier typically takes to deliver, and a safety stock buffer for demand spikes or delivery delays, and this calculator gives you the exact unit count that should trigger your next order.

items.question: What is the reorder point formula?
items.answer: Reorder Point = (Average Daily Sales × Supplier Lead Time in Days) + Safety Stock. It's the stock level at which a new order should be placed so the replacement stock arrives before you run out.

items.question: How do I decide on a safety stock number?
items.answer: Safety stock should cover both demand variability (busier-than-average days) and supplier delays. A common starting approach is a few days' worth of average sales, adjusted up for items with unreliable suppliers or volatile demand.

items.question: What if my supplier's lead time varies a lot?
items.answer: Use your worst realistic lead time rather than the best case when calculating the reorder point, and lean on a larger safety stock buffer for items where lead time is unpredictable.

items.question: Does this account for seasonal demand spikes?
items.answer: Not directly — this uses your average daily sales figure. For seasonal items, recalculate using the average daily sales expected during the upcoming season rather than a flat annual average.

cta.headline: Want early access to Setu Retail?
cta.subtext: Tell us about your retail business and we'll notify you as soon as Setu Retail launches, with reorder alerts built in.

items.name: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find out exactly how much you're spending to acquire each new customer.

seo.title: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) Calculator | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free CAC calculator. Enter marketing spend and new customers acquired to find your customer acquisition cost, and compare it against revenue per customer.

hero.headline: Customer Acquisition Cost Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter your marketing spend and new customers acquired to find your cost per customer.

about.headline: The number that tells you if marketing is working
paragraphs: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is the average amount you spend to win one new customer, across all the marketing and sales costs that went into acquiring them. It only means something next to a second number — what that customer is actually worth to you.

Enter your total spend over a period and the number of new customers it brought in to get your CAC. If you also know the average revenue per customer, this calculator shows the ratio between the two — a quick gut-check on whether a campaign or channel is actually paying for itself.

items.question: What is the CAC formula?
items.answer: CAC = Total Marketing and Sales Spend ÷ Number of New Customers Acquired, over the same period. Spending ₹50,000 to acquire 100 new customers gives a CAC of ₹500.

items.question: What costs should I include in marketing spend?
items.answer: Include ad spend, agency or platform fees, promotional discounts used purely for acquisition, and a reasonable share of sales team cost if relevant — anything spent specifically to bring in new customers during that period.

items.question: What's a good CAC?
items.answer: It depends entirely on what a customer is worth to you over time. A CAC is healthy if it's comfortably below the revenue (ideally repeat revenue) a typical customer generates — there's no fixed 'good' number across businesses.

items.question: How is CAC different from average order value?
items.answer: CAC measures what you spent to win a customer; average order value measures what a customer spends per order once acquired. Comparing the two tells you roughly how many orders it takes to recover your acquisition cost.

cta.headline: Know which customers are worth keeping
cta.subtext: Setu POS tracks repeat customers and order history, so you can see which channels bring in customers who actually come back.

items.name: Average Order Value (AOV) Calculator
items.shortDescription: Find your average order value, and see the revenue impact of nudging it up.

seo.title: Average Order Value (AOV) Calculator | Setu Technology
seo.description: Free AOV calculator. Enter total revenue and number of orders to find your average order value, and see the revenue impact of increasing it.

hero.headline: Average Order Value Calculator
hero.subheadline: Enter total revenue and number of orders to find your AOV, and see what a small increase is worth.

about.headline: A small AOV increase compounds fast
paragraphs: Average order value (AOV) is the average amount spent per order — and it's one of the few revenue levers you can move without acquiring a single new customer. A combo offer, a well-placed add-on suggestion, or a small price adjustment can lift AOV across every order you already get.

Enter your total revenue and number of orders for a period to find your current AOV, then enter a target percentage increase to see what that lift would be worth in extra revenue at the same order volume — useful for justifying whether an upsell push or menu/bundle change is worth the effort.

items.question: What is the average order value formula?
items.answer: AOV = Total Revenue ÷ Number of Orders, for the same period. ₹5,00,000 in revenue across 1,000 orders gives an AOV of ₹500.

items.question: How can I increase average order value?
items.answer: Common levers include combo or bundle offers, upselling a larger size or add-on at the point of order, minimum-spend free delivery thresholds, and prominently suggesting complementary items alongside what a customer is already ordering.

items.question: Why does a small AOV increase matter so much?
items.answer: Because it applies to every order you already get, with no additional acquisition cost — a 10% AOV lift on your existing order volume drops straight to extra revenue, without needing a single new customer.

items.question: Is a higher AOV always better?
items.answer: Generally yes for revenue, but check it doesn't come at the cost of order frequency or customer satisfaction — pushing add-ons too aggressively can backfire. Track AOV alongside repeat order rate to catch that early.

cta.headline: Spot upsell opportunities automatically
cta.subtext: Setu POS surfaces combo and add-on prompts at billing, so lifting AOV doesn't rely on staff remembering to ask.
